name: Calibrated Questions: Make Them Solve Your Problem
description: Open-ended questions starting with 'how' or 'what' that force the counterparty to do the cognitive work — and frequently to invent the solution you wanted. The single highest-ROI verbal move in negotiation.
disclaimer: Calibrated questions are a power tool. Used clumsily ('how am I supposed to do that?' as a whine instead of a real question) they sound passive-aggressive. Tone must be genuinely curious.

context.when_to_use: Any time you would otherwise make a counter-argument, an accusation, or a demand. Substitute a calibrated question instead. Most valuable when the counterparty has just said 'no', 'I can't', or 'that's not possible'.
preconditions: You have at least one calibrated question pre-loaded for the likely objections.
You can deliver the question with curious-flat tone — not whiny, not aggressive.
You have patience for silence after the question (3-8 seconds is normal).

principles: Start with 'how' or 'what'. Never 'why' — 'why' triggers defensiveness across every language. 'Why did you do that?' = accusation. 'What led to that decision?' = invitation.
Avoid yes/no questions entirely. Yes is non-committal; no is a wall; calibrated questions produce information.
The best calibrated questions make THEIR no MY problem to solve — and force them to either help solve it or admit there's room.
Calibrated questions slow the call down. That asymmetry favors the patient party — which is you, if you've prepared.
After asking, do not soften, qualify, or rephrase. The question must stand alone in the silence.
Use calibrated questions to gather, then to commit: 'How will we know it's working?' converts a verbal agreement into an implementation plan.

phrases.core_how_am_i: How am I supposed to do that?
phrases.core_what_would_it_take: What would it take to get there?
phrases.discover_constraints: What's the biggest challenge you face in approving something like this?
phrases.discover_authority: Who on your end has handled an exception like this before?
phrases.discover_process: How does this normally work for customers in my situation?
phrases.discover_value: What does success look like for you on a call like this?
phrases.test_a_no: What about it doesn't work for you?
phrases.expand_the_pie: What else might we put on the table that would make this easier?
phrases.force_creativity: If price were fixed, what else could move?
phrases.commitment_check: How will we make sure this actually happens after we hang up?

tactics.name: Substitute, don't argue
tactics.when: You feel the urge to say 'but…' or 'that's not fair'.
tactics.script: Replace the argument with a calibrated question. 'But I've been a customer for 8 years!' becomes 'How does loyalty factor in to what you can offer?'

tactics.name: Convert no into a workshop
tactics.script: Respond with `test_a_no`. They will tell you the specific obstacle. Address THAT, not the global no.

tactics.name: Outsource creativity
tactics.when: You're stuck and don't have a next move.
tactics.script: Use `force_creativity` or `expand_the_pie`. The rep often invents the workaround for you — and people defend ideas that came out of their own mouth.

tactics.name: Surface the real decision-maker
tactics.when: Rep keeps citing policy or authority limits.
tactics.script: Use `discover_authority`. Not 'can I talk to your supervisor' (combative) but 'who has handled this kind of exception' (curious). Different door, same room.

tactics.name: Lock in the deal with an implementation question
tactics.script: Use `commitment_check`. Forces the rep to spell out the system actions, the timing, and the confirmation — converting talk into commitment and exposing any quietly-missing steps.

decision_tree.description: When the counterparty produces a blocking move, match it to a branch BEFORE you respond. Calibrated questions are interruption-proof — you can deliver one inside 1.5 seconds of pause.

branches.interpretation: Wall is rhetorical, not actual. The rep is testing if you'll fold.
branches.say: `test_a_no`: 'What about it doesn't work for you?'
branches.do_not: Do not accept the no. Do not argue with the no. Question it.

branches.interpretation: Either truth or a polite final-offer feint. The question separates them.
branches.say: `core_how_am_i`: 'How am I supposed to make this work on a [budget/target] of X?'
branches.do_not: Do not name a counter-number. Make THEM produce one.

branches.say: `discover_authority`: 'Who on your end has handled an exception like this before?'
branches.do_not: Do not demand a supervisor. Ask who has done it before — names route you differently than escalations.

branches.if_you_hear: Long script delivered with no breaks.
branches.interpretation: Rep is on rails. The script is designed to terminate negotiation.
branches.say: Interrupt gently at the next breath: 'Quick question — `discover_process`: how does this normally work for customers in my situation?'
branches.do_not: Do not wait for the script to end. The end of the script is the call-end button.

branches.if_you_hear: 'What's your budget / what were you hoping for?'
branches.interpretation: They are asking YOU to anchor. Dangerous if you haven't decided.
branches.say: Deflect with a calibrated question: 'What would be a typical range for someone in my situation? I want to make sure I'm framing this fairly.'
branches.do_not: Do not name a number first if you can avoid it. See `03-anchor-and-counter-anchor`.

branches.interpretation: Ultimatum — almost always a bluff in commercial contexts.
branches.say: `core_what_would_it_take`: 'What would it take to move off that position?' delivered curious, not hostile.
branches.do_not: Do not flinch. Do not accept. Do not threaten to leave (that's a separate skill — see `10-walkaway-threats`).

branches.if_you_hear: Genuine creative offer ('what if we did X instead?').
branches.say: `force_creativity`: 'I like where that's going — what else might we put on the table to bridge the rest of the gap?'
branches.do_not: Do not jump to accept the first creative offer — there's usually a better one one question deeper.

branches.interpretation: Risky moment — the deal isn't real until it's specified.
branches.say: `commitment_check`: 'How will we make sure this actually happens after we hang up?'
branches.do_not: Do not hang up on a verbal yes. Verbal yes + no implementation question = no deal in 50% of cases.

boundaries: Do NOT ask 'why' questions. Substitute 'what led to' or 'how did you get there'.
Do NOT chain 4+ calibrated questions in a row — it starts to feel like an interrogation.
Do NOT use 'how am I supposed to do that?' as sarcasm. It must be curious or it fails.
Do NOT ask a calibrated question and then answer it yourself in the silence. Wait.
success_signals: Rep produces a workaround or exception you hadn't named.
Rep says 'good question, let me check' — they're now solving your problem.
Rep volunteers the name of the actual decision-maker.
Rep starts asking calibrated questions back at you — engagement is mutual.
Silence after your question lasts 5+ seconds — they're thinking, which is what you want.
failure_signals: Rep robotically repeats the original no after 3 calibrated questions — you may be talking to someone with truly zero authority; escalate.
Rep gets visibly annoyed by the question pattern — your tone may have drifted to combative; reset with a label.
You can't think of the next calibrated question — switch to labeling (`01-tactical-empathy-labeling`) to buy time.

exit_strategy.on_success: Once the rep produces the solution, confirm with `commitment_check` and exit cleanly.
exit_strategy.on_failure: If 3+ calibrated questions don't move the call, the rep likely has neither authority nor latitude. Politely close, log the attempt, escalate via different channel.
follow_ups: Save the calibrated questions that worked for this provider/industry — patterns repeat.
Note which questions produced new information vs. which were dead ends.

required_user_info: What the user actually wants (the question is calibrated to a goal — you need the goal).
Any specific constraints the user has (budget, timing) so questions don't accidentally over-promise.
User's tolerance for silence — some operators want to be coached in real time to NOT rescue the silence.
